failure_modes.text: Teams can blame every bad outcome on distorted incentives even when uncertainty, luck, or weak capability are the dominant drivers.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when every poor outcome gets attributed to moral hazard even when the dominant driver is uncertainty, bad luck, or weak capability rather than distorted incentives.
failure_modes.mitigation: Diagnose the real driver before invoking incentive distortion: check whether the outcome is better explained by uncertainty, capability limits, or system design before blaming hidden behavior.

failure_modes.text: Outcome-based contracts can misfire when observable results are shaped heavily by chance rather than by the hidden behavior being targeted.
failure_modes.source_quote: In reality, outcomes are dependent not just on the agent's action/effort, but also on chance factors.
failure_modes.mitigation: When outcomes are noisy, shift from pure outcome-based contracts to process-observable metrics that reward the behavior you can actually verify.

failure_modes.text: Immediate outcome incentives can create short-termism that externalizes longer-term risk.
failure_modes.source_quote: If payments are based solely on immediate observable outcomes ... this can inadvertently incentivize short-term gains at the expense of long-term stability or ethical behavior.
failure_modes.mitigation: Add longer time horizons or clawback provisions to contracts so agents cannot externalize tail risk by cashing out before consequences arrive.

premortem_questions.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask: “Is the worst case bad enough?”.

premortem_questions.text: Who takes the action, and who absorbs the downside if it goes wrong?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when one party can take action while another party absorbs a meaningful share of the downside.

premortem_questions.text: Are accountability, exposure, and decision rights aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best used when the question is whether accountability, exposure, and decision rights are aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior.

heuristics.text: Structure incentives so people experience enough consequence from their decisions to behave like owners rather than insulated agents.
heuristics.source_quote: Managers should structure incentives and penalties to encourage people to take full ownership of what they do.

heuristics.text: Discuss failures at both the machine level and the case-at-hand level so the fix targets the system that produced the behavior.
heuristics.source_quote: When a problem occurs, always discuss it at two levels: 1) the machine level ... and 2) the case-at-hand level.

heuristics.text: Welcome probing and peer review because observers often detect hidden risk-taking logic better than the actors inside it.
heuristics.source_quote: Observers (critics and gossipers) are often more effective at detecting errors than actors, suggesting the use of peer review or a brain trust to catch blind spots.

curation_notes.summary: The source is naturally chunk-ready on downside transfer, ownership alignment, and anti-blame discipline around chance and capability.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor governance sprawl because the raw source is stronger on agency, exposure alignment, and peer-probing countermeasures.
open_questions: The third heuristic compresses the broader critique/observer passages so it remains reusable outside the original organizational examples.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on incentives, information asymmetry, and system-level accountability for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section is strongest on inside-view rationalization, availability-shaped misweighting of risk, and emotional shortcuts.
structured_tensions: The source includes an explicit structured tension block around adverse-selection and incentives that cleanly maps into the current relation layer vocabulary.
